record.body: in the daniela.lol app there is a toggle to disable the superfluous up arrow on the lower left of feeds. it behaves like the home button, so is redundant, and i find it visually distracting. it's honestly the only reason i still use an old daniela.lol build instead of this app
record.title: feat: toggle up arrow on feed

record.body:
i need to be able to mute some people for some of the time.

sometimes a person is either reskeeting or just skeeting about something tiresome for a few days and then they stop, and i want to be able to temporarily mute them for that time.

i also want to be able to ONLY mute reskeets on an offending-user-level basis so if someone posts bangers but reskeets tons of porn or doomer articles i can just disable their reskeets without unfollowing

i need this because sometimes i dont want to entirely mute someone because we are mutuals and i want to be able to see their replies to me, but i need them to not be filling my feed with news articles that make me want to jump off a bridge, but i cant escape without breaking mutual or muting the entire account.

i think both currently available options are extreme and unnecessary in a lot of cases and we need more gradation in our options. some people i want to mute temporarily and some people i want to mute reskeets and some people i want every combination thereof 😮‍💨

i know i can disable all reskeets wholesale but i dont think i should have to demolish half my feed just because one person reskeets stuff i dont wanna see, i should be able to mute the specific user just like on twitter. why are we behind TWITTER on this point?!
record.title: deep mute settings

record.body:
Add a single global cadence clock for the whole feed list, distinct from
per-feed eligibility. `refresh_due()` (pure, deterministic — takes `now`)
decides when an automatic full-list refresh is due, anchored on a durable
`last_refresh` stored machine-locally in the per-store cache dir so a synced
store copy never conflicts on a value that changes every cadence.

- turss-domain: `refresh_due()` + unit tests (never-refreshed / within-window
  / past-window / boundary / unparseable cases).
- turss-store: `load_last_refresh` / `save_last_refresh` on the Store trait,
  the fsstore impl, and store tests.
- turss-runtime/queue: arm the automatic refresh from the cadence clock.
- flake: drop the devshell shellHook echo.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
record.title: Move reindexing out of the UI and into a separate cli command

record.body:
`turss reindex` re-embedded the whole captured set one article at a time —
load+parse (CPU) → embed (Metal) → upsert (redb), each awaited in series — so
the GPU idled during every parse and write, and each embed call carried only one
article's handful of chunks. On a large store the full rebuild took hours.

Refactor `index_article` into three shared, blocking-pool stage helpers
(`load_and_chunk` / `embed_chunks` / `upsert_pairs`); the per-fetch path is now a
thin composition of them with identical behavior.

Replace the sequential reindex loop with a pipeline that overlaps the three
stages across articles:
- a parse-ahead producer streams `(id, chunks)` over a bounded channel so CPU
  parsing runs ahead of the GPU,
- a batch-embed driver accumulates whole articles up to a soft chunk cap
  (REINDEX_BATCH_CHUNKS=64) and embeds each batch in one call, splitting the
  vectors back per article, and
- upserts run on a bounded JoinSet so redb writes overlap the next batch's embed.

Correctness is preserved: a batch-embed error (or length mismatch) falls back to
per-article embedding so one bad article can't sink the batch; per-article upsert
failures count only themselves; the model stamp is still advanced only on a clean
pass; progress is reported from a single drain point, ending at (total, total).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
record.title: perf(index): pipeline and batch the reindex loop

record.body:
## Summary

 Implement the `createRecord` XRPC endpoint for creating new records in a user's repository.

 ## Changes

 - `POST /xrpc/com.atproto.repo.createRecord`
 - Accepts `repo` (DID), `collection` (NSID), optional `rkey`, and `record` data in body
 - Auto-generates TID (13-char base32-sortable timestamp identifier) when `rkey` not provided
 - Validates collection/rkey format before touching repo
 - Authenticated: requires Bearer token matching repo DID
 - Returns AT URI and record CID per spec
 - Optimistic concurrency via `repo_root_cid` CAS (compare-and-swap)
 - Best-effort post-commit block GC (reclaims superseded blocks)

 ## TID Generation

 Implements ATProto Timestamp Identifiers:
 - 64-bit integer: 0 | microseconds-since-epoch (52 bits) | random-clock-id (10 bits)
 - Encoded as 13-char base32-sortable string (`234567abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz`)
 - Monotonically increasing, collision-resistant

 ## Acceptance Criteria

 - [x] Authenticated POST creates record and returns AT URI + CID
 - [x] Record retrievable via getRecord after creation
 - [x] Repo head advances to new commit
 - [x] Unauthorized repo access returns 403

 ## Tests

 10 new tests covering:
 - Auth: 401 without token, 403 for wrong DID
 - Explicit rkey: returns correct URI and CID
 - Auto-generated TID: 13-char base32-sortable format
 - Validation: invalid collection, floats rejected
 - Not found: nonexistent account returns 404
 - Roundtrip: created record retrievable via getRecord
 - TID unit tests: valid format, monotonicity
record.title: relay: add com.atproto.repo.createRecord (MM-100)

record.body:
I reported this bug previously by email in April, and it was fixed, but somehow, it has now regressed.

When non-browser agents (e.g. web crawlers and headless fetchers) access posts via my custom domain they are getting a 401 error, while the same post loads fine via the leaflet.pub subdomain URL.

Adding ?auth_completed=true to the end of the URL makes it accessible, but that doesn't really help since ?auth_completed=true is not in the canonical URL. A non-browser agent (or even a browser agent) should not need to be authenticated to access public content. Also, if simply adding that to the end of the URL makes it work, then whatever this auth_completed thing is, it is not actually an authentication flow.

This is particularly problematic because a custom domain becomes the canonical URL, even for the leaflet.pub subdomain version. Therefore, at this point, adding a custom domain, rather than enhancing a Leaflet publication, completely breaks SEO and anything else that requires fetching through anything other than a browser. It's paradoxical that adding an enhancement degrades the functionality.

I have tested this with other Leaflet publications that use custom domains and the behaviour is the same, so it's not an issue with my own DNS setup. Ultimately, request handling should transparently be the same for both the custom domain and the leaflet.pub subdomain.
record.title: Regression: Discrepancy in request handling for custom domains with non-browser agents

record.description: Online vulpine.

record.body:
I reported this bug previously by email in April, and it was fixed, but somehow, it has now regressed.

When non-browser agents (e.g. web crawlers and headless fetchers) access posts via my custom domain they are getting a 401 error, while the same post loads fine via the leaflet.pub subdomain URL.

Adding ?auth_completed=true to the end of the URL makes it accessible, but that doesn't really help since ?auth_completed=true is not in the canonical URL. A non-browser agent (or even a browser agent) should not need to be authenticated to access public content. Also, if simply adding that to the end of the URL makes it work, then whatever this auth_completed thing is, it is not actually an authentication flow.

This is particularly problematic because a custom domain becomes the canonical URL, even for the leaflet.pub subdomain version. Therefore, at this point, adding a custom domain, rather than enhancing a Leaflet publication, completely breaks SEO and anything else that requires fetching through anything other than a browser. It's paradoxical that adding an enhancement degrades the functionality.

I have tested this with other Leaflet publications that use custom domains and the behaviour is the same, so it's not an issue with my own DNS setup. Ultimately, request handling should transparently be the same for both the custom domain and the leaflet.pub subdomain.

Here is the commit where this was fixed the first time:
https://tangled.org/leaflet.pub/leaflet/commit/3ebf9823d5c995b8e2bc8240b3a11ec8cf7abbe7

Though to be honest, I think this patch was treating the symptom rather than the problem, so it's no wonder that it has regressed.

You shouldn't need an exception that lets bots through, because there shouldn't even be a thing that they need to be given an exception to. A public page shouldn't just be straight up broken for anything that can't load Javascript or cookies. They should at least get something.
record.title: Regression: Discrepancy in request handling for custom domains with non-browser agents

record.body:
I reported this bug previously by email in April, and the fix that was applied has not quite addressed the issue.

When non-browser agents (e.g. web crawlers and headless fetchers) access posts via my custom domain they are getting a 401 error, while the same post loads fine via the leaflet.pub subdomain URL. The fix added a regex for specific bot useragents, but that's a rather fragile fix that doesn't really address the underlying issue.

Adding ?auth_completed=true to the end of the URL makes it accessible, but that doesn't really help since ?auth_completed=true is not in the canonical URL. A non-browser agent (or even a browser agent) should not need to be authenticated to access public content. Also, if simply adding that to the end of the URL makes it work, then whatever this auth_completed thing is, it is not actually an authentication flow.

This is particularly problematic because a custom domain becomes the canonical URL, even for the leaflet.pub subdomain version. Therefore, at this point, adding a custom domain, rather than enhancing a Leaflet publication, completely breaks SEO and anything else that requires fetching through anything other than a browser. It's paradoxical that adding an enhancement degrades the functionality.

I have tested this with other Leaflet publications that use custom domains and the behaviour is the same, so it's not an issue with my own DNS setup. Ultimately, request handling should transparently be the same for both the custom domain and the leaflet.pub subdomain.

Here is the commit where this was addressed the first time around:
https://tangled.org/leaflet.pub/leaflet/commit/3ebf9823d5c995b8e2bc8240b3a11ec8cf7abbe7

I think this patch was treating the symptom rather than the problem.

You shouldn't need an exception that lets bots through, because there shouldn't even be a thing that they need to be given an exception to. A public page shouldn't just be straight up broken for anything that can't load Javascript or cookies. They should at least get something.
record.title: Discrepancy in request handling for custom domains with non-browser agents

record.body:
`repo.Did` should be a DID of repository itself. Git repos are not tied to their current owner, so using `.Did` field for owner DID is not correct.

Same goes to `repo.Rkey`. Git repos are not a record, they are standalone identity and the source of truth is git repository DID, not record in someone's PDS.

| old | new |
|-|-|
| `repo.RepoDid` | `repo.Did` |
| `repo.Did` | `repo.Owner` |
| `repo.Rkey` | `repo.Slug` or `repo.Name` |

For xrpc method or record definition,

When we _reference_ a git repository (e.g. `sh.tangled.feed.star`) -> name field as `repo` with type string, DID format.

When we _represent_ a git repository (e.g. `sh.tangled.sync.listRepos`) -> name field as `did` since the object itself is already a "repo". It should be `.repo.did` instead of `.repo.repo`.
record.title: rename `repo.Did` to `repo.Owner`

record.body:
`repo.Did` should be a DID of repository itself. Git repos are not tied to their current owner, so using `.Did` field for owner DID is not correct.

Same goes to `repo.Rkey`. Git repos are not a record, they are standalone identity and the source of truth is git repository DID, not record in someone's PDS.

| old | new |
|-|-|
| `repo.RepoDid` | `repo.Did` |
| `repo.Did` | `repo.Owner` |
| `repo.Rkey` | `repo.Slug` or `repo.Name` |

For xrpc method or record definition,

When we _reference_ a git repository (e.g. `sh.tangled.feed.star`) -> name the field as `repo` with type string, DID format.

When we _represent_ a git repository (e.g. `sh.tangled.sync.listRepos`) -> name the field as `did` since the object itself is already a "repo". It should be `.repo.did` instead of `.repo.repo`.
record.title: rename `repo.Did` to `repo.Owner`
